Not only are the costs of turnover likely to be damaging in a dishonest organization, there are severe follow-on charges as well. That is, in such organizations, turnover will not occur equally among those employees who are and who are not comfortable with cheating. Those troubled by the cheating will leave, but those comfortable with it to achieve their financial gains should be happy to stay. The organizational implication is clear and sobering. There are several influence practices that excuse cheating will select for and retain cheaters. And this is important, who are likely to cheat the organization. By our argument then, an unethical enterprise will draw under its coat a viper, a snake whose bite will be fiscally venomous as the costs of employee fraud and malfeasance are figured in trillions of dollars worldwide, stemming from embezzlement, theft of inventory or equipment, expense account inflation, falsified purchasing reports, and under-the-table deals with vendors or business partners. In a final set of studies, our team found exactly what we had expected. Employees in unethical organizations who preferred to remain in them were abnormally likely to engage in deceitful, financially costly workplace activity, such as altering expensive reports, sabotaging equipment to avoid having to work that day, and using company resources for private purposes. Thus, individuals happy to reside in unethical workplace nests were also happy to follow all those nests.
